title: WOUND DEHISCENCE with EVISCERATION on Postoperative Day 5 in a 68-Year-Old Man After Exploratory Laparotomy and Sigmoid Colectomy for Complicated Diverticulitis (Risk Factors: Type 2 Diabetes with A1c 8.4, Obesity BMI 34, Former Heavy Smoking, Postoperative Ileus with Persistent Coughing, Clean-Contaminated Wound) — The Classic 'Popping' Sensation During a Forceful Cough with Visible BOWEL LOOPS Through the Incision: Recognizing the Emergency, IMMEDIATE NURSING RESPONSE (STAY With Patient, LOW-Fowler's With KNEES FLEXED, Cover with STERILE NORMAL-SALINE-MOISTENED Gauze, DO NOT Push Viscera Back, KEEP MOIST, NPO), STAT Surgeon Notification + IV Access + Isotonic Fluid + Labs + Type & Screen + Broad-Spectrum IV Antibiotics, Coordinated Emergent Return to OR for Fascial Repair, and Postoperative Care + Recurrence-Prevention Teaching (Abdominal Binder, SPLINTING, Stool Softeners, Glycemic and Nutritional Optimization)
subtitle: WOUND DEHISCENCE is partial or complete SEPARATION of the layers of a surgical wound (skin and/or fascia/muscle); EVISCERATION is PROTRUSION OF VISCERA (typically intestine) through the wound — a true SURGICAL EMERGENCY. The CLASSIC patient story is a 'POPPING' or 'GIVING WAY' sensation during a forceful cough, sneeze, retch, or strain on POSTOPERATIVE DAY 5-12 (the window after sutures are tested by mobilization and before full tensile strength is restored — earlier suggests technical failure; later suggests infection/poor healing), with sudden warm wet drainage at the incision, often serosanguineous or 'salmon-colored,' followed by visible separation of the wound edges and, in evisceration, EXPOSED BOWEL LOOPS. RISK FACTORS cluster around impaired healing or increased intra-abdominal pressure: ADVANCED AGE; MALNUTRITION (low albumin/prealbumin, vitamin C and zinc deficiency); DIABETES with poor glycemic control (microangiopathy, neutrophil dysfunction); OBESITY (poor wound perfusion, mechanical stress); IMMUNOSUPPRESSION (chronic corticosteroids, chemotherapy, transplant medications); SMOKING (vasoconstriction, hypoxia); SURGICAL SITE INFECTION (the single most modifiable post-op factor); INCREASED INTRA-ABDOMINAL PRESSURE (coughing, sneezing, vomiting, straining at stool, ileus, ascites, abdominal distention); HEMATOMA/seroma; emergency surgery or contamination; repeated incisions through the same site; and technical factors. IMMEDIATE NURSING ACTIONS — high-yield NCLEX — are choreographed and must NOT be delayed by 'going to get supplies' or 'pushing it back in': (1) STAY WITH THE PATIENT and CALL FOR HELP — do not leave the bedside, do not try to manage alone; (2) POSITION the patient in LOW-FOWLER'S (about 15-20 degrees of head elevation) with KNEES FLEXED on a pillow — this RELAXES the abdominal wall and REDUCES intra-abdominal pressure on the defect; AVOID high-Fowler's, sitting upright, or flat with legs extended (all increase tension on the wound); (3) COVER the exposed viscera with STERILE GAUZE MOISTENED with sterile NORMAL SALINE and KEEP IT MOIST throughout — re-moisten with sterile saline as needed; (4) DO NOT push the viscera back into the abdomen (causes contamination, peritonitis, mesenteric injury, and ischemia), DO NOT use DRY gauze (the bowel will dry/necrose), DO NOT use cold dressings or apply povidone-iodine/hydrogen peroxide/tap water to the bowel; (5) NPO immediately (anticipating the OR); (6) NOTIFY THE SURGEON STAT — and the rapid-response/OR team per facility policy; (7) ESTABLISH/secure two large-bore IVs and run isotonic fluid (typically NS or LR) per orders; (8) SEND labs (CBC, BMP, coagulation, type and screen) and INITIATE broad-spectrum IV antibiotics per the surgeon (contamination/infection risk); (9) MONITOR vital signs frequently and watch for SHOCK (tachycardia, hypotension), peritonitis (rigidity, rebound, guarding), and worsening; (10) PREPARE FOR THE OR — confirm consent for the emergency repair, hold transport, alert anesthesia, and document the time of the event, the precipitating event (cough/strain), the wound findings, interventions, vital signs, and response. Pain control is given per surgeon order — never delay the surgical call to medicate for pain. DEFINITIVE management is EMERGENT RETURN TO THE OR for surgical repair: re-approximation of the fascia (often through-and-through sutures or retention sutures), peritoneal washout, hemostasis, and, depending on tissue quality and defect size, possible mesh placement; broad-spectrum antibiotics continue; post-operatively the patient often returns with NG decompression, an abdominal binder, and a slowed mobilization plan, with close watch for re-dehiscence, surgical site infection, and ileus. PREVENTION focuses on the modifiable: pre-operative optimization (nutrition, glycemia, smoking cessation), abdominal binders for high-risk patients, SPLINTING the incision with a pillow during cough/sneeze/vomit/bowel movement, treating ileus and managing post-op nausea (antiemetics), STOOL SOFTENERS to prevent straining, careful incentive spirometry technique (deep breathing without bracing the wound), early but graded mobilization, and recognizing/treating surgical site infection promptly. PERTINENT MISTAKES TO AVOID: pushing viscera back into the abdomen; using DRY gauze, ice, tap water, povidone-iodine, or hydrogen peroxide on the bowel; positioning the patient high-Fowler's or sitting upright or flat with legs extended; leaving the patient alone to fetch supplies; giving oral fluids or food; delaying the surgeon call to perform a full charting or to medicate first; and forgetting to document the precipitating event and timeline. NURSING ROLE: recognize the classic 'popping/sudden drainage' presentation; stay with the patient and call for help; position low-Fowler's with knees flexed; cover the exposed viscera with sterile saline-moistened gauze and keep it moist; NPO; notify the surgeon STAT; establish IV access and labs/antibiotics; monitor for shock; prepare for the OR; and after repair coordinate prevention-focused post-operative care and teaching.

patient.name: J. Larsen
patient.code: Full code; postoperative surgical patient now in an acute wound-emergency state (dehiscence with evisceration); goal = immediately recognize the emergency, stay with the patient, position low-Fowler's with knees flexed, cover viscera with sterile saline-moistened gauze (kept moist; never pushed back), NPO, notify the surgeon STAT, establish IV access + fluids + labs + antibiotics, prepare for emergent OR, and coordinate post-operative recovery and recurrence-prevention teaching
patient.admitDate: Postoperative day 5 from an exploratory laparotomy with sigmoid colectomy and primary anastomosis for complicated diverticulitis with abscess (clean-contaminated wound). PMH: T2DM with A1c 8.4 (mildly hyperglycemic perioperatively), obesity (BMI 34), former 40-pack-year smoker who quit 10 years ago; nutrition is borderline (low prealbumin/albumin). Post-op course complicated by mild ileus with persistent intermittent COUGHING from atelectasis. Diet advanced to clear liquids. During a forceful coughing fit at 1015 he reported a sudden POPPING sensation at the midline laparotomy incision with a warm wet drainage soaking the dressing; on assessment the incision had SEPARATED and loops of BOWEL are protruding through the fascial defect = EVISCERATION, a surgical emergency. Today's RN focus: the choreographed immediate response, coordination of the emergent OR return, and post-op recurrence prevention.
patient.room: General med-surg unit; post-op bay; vascular access available; surgeon being paged; OR + anesthesia + general surgery team being mobilized; family being notified

vitals.temp: 37.6°C; sudden popping sensation at midline laparotomy incision during a forceful coughing fit; warm wet 'salmon-colored' drainage soaking the dressing; pain at incision; patient anxious and frightened

37.6°C; on dressing removal, the incision has SEPARATED and several loops of BOWEL are protruding through the fascial defect = EVISCERATION; surgical emergency; the nurse remains at the bedside and calls for help

vitals.time: Immediate intervention — position + sterile moist gauze + surgeon STAT
vitals.temp: 37.5°C; positioned LOW-FOWLER'S (about 20 degrees) with KNEES FLEXED on a pillow (reduces abdominal-wall tension); exposed viscera covered with STERILE NORMAL-SALINE-MOISTENED gauze, kept MOIST (re-moistened with sterile saline as needed); DO NOT push viscera back; NPO; surgeon notified STAT; rapid-response/OR team activated

vitals.time: Stabilization — IV access + fluids + labs + antibiotic + consent
vitals.temp: 37.5°C; two large-bore IVs established; isotonic IV fluid running; labs sent (CBC, BMP, coags, type and screen); broad-spectrum IV antibiotic given per surgeon order (contamination risk); informed consent for emergency wound repair signed; transport to OR arranged

37.1°C; emergent return to OR; fascial repair with retention/through-and-through sutures + peritoneal washout + hemostasis (mesh deferred given clean-contaminated field); IV antibiotics continued; NG tube placed for decompression; abdominal binder applied; transferred to PACU then back to the unit

37.0°C; recovering on the unit; nutrition consult (protein/vit C/zinc); glycemic control tightened (insulin sliding scale + basal optimization); SPLINTING with cough/sneeze/BM taught and practiced; abdominal binder for activity; stool softeners; graded mobilization; no lifting > 10 lb for 6-8 weeks at discharge; warning signs of recurrence/infection reviewed; follow-up scheduled

labs.n: PRESENTATION + dehiscence vs evisceration + risk factors + classic POD 5-12 timing
labs.v: 68 y/o man J. Larsen, POD 5 from exploratory laparotomy + sigmoid colectomy for complicated diverticulitis (clean-contaminated). RISK FACTORS for poor wound healing: T2DM with A1c 8.4 (perioperative hyperglycemia), obesity (BMI 34), former heavy smoker, borderline nutrition (low prealbumin/albumin), post-op ileus with persistent cough (raises intra-abdominal pressure). During a forceful coughing fit at 1015 he reported a sudden POPPING sensation at the midline incision with warm wet 'salmon-colored' drainage; on assessment the incision had SEPARATED and BOWEL loops were protruding through the fascial defect = WOUND DEHISCENCE WITH EVISCERATION. DEHISCENCE = partial/complete separation of wound layers (skin and/or fascia/muscle); EVISCERATION = protrusion of viscera through the wound — more emergent than dehiscence alone. CLASSIC TIMING is postoperative day 5-12 (sutures stressed by mobilization, full tensile strength not yet reached); earlier suggests technical failure, later suggests infection/poor healing. The nursing task: an immediate, choreographed response and coordination of the emergent OR return — with recurrence-prevention teaching afterward.
labs.r: Dehiscence = wound-layer separation; EVISCERATION = viscera through the wound (more emergent). Classic 'popping' on cough/strain on POD 5-12 + 'salmon-colored' drainage. Risks: age, DM (poor A1c), obesity, malnutrition (low albumin/prealbumin), smoking, immunosuppression, SSI, ↑intra-abdominal pressure (cough/strain/ileus/ascites/distension), repeat incisions, emergency/contaminated cases. RN role: recognize the popping + drainage cue + visualize evisceration.

labs.n: IMMEDIATE nursing response (position + sterile saline-moist gauze + NPO + surgeon STAT)
labs.v: The immediate response is choreographed and must NOT be delayed. (1) STAY WITH the patient and CALL FOR HELP — do not leave the bedside to fetch supplies and do not try to manage alone. (2) POSITION the patient LOW-FOWLER'S (about 15-20 degrees) with KNEES FLEXED on a pillow — this RELAXES the abdominal wall and REDUCES intra-abdominal pressure on the defect; AVOID high-Fowler's, sitting upright, or flat with legs extended (all increase tension and worsen evisceration). (3) COVER the exposed viscera with STERILE GAUZE MOISTENED with sterile NORMAL SALINE and KEEP IT MOIST throughout (re-moisten with sterile saline as needed). (4) DO NOT push the viscera back into the abdomen (causes contamination, peritonitis, mesenteric injury, ischemia); DO NOT use DRY gauze (bowel will dry/necrose); DO NOT apply cold dressings, povidone-iodine, hydrogen peroxide, or tap water to the bowel. (5) NPO immediately (anticipating OR). (6) NOTIFY THE SURGEON STAT and activate rapid-response/OR per facility policy. (7) Establish two LARGE-BORE IVs; run isotonic fluid (NS or LR) per orders; send labs (CBC, BMP, coags, type and screen); give a BROAD-SPECTRUM IV ANTIBIOTIC per surgeon order (contamination/infection risk). (8) Frequent vital signs — watch for SHOCK (tachycardia, hypotension), PERITONITIS (rigidity, rebound, guarding), and worsening status. (9) PREPARE for the OR — confirm INFORMED CONSENT for the emergency repair, alert anesthesia, arrange transport. (10) DOCUMENT the time of the event, the precipitating event (cough/strain), wound findings, every intervention, vital signs, and patient response. Pain control is given per surgeon order — never delay the surgical call to medicate first.
labs.r: (1) STAY + call for help; (2) LOW-FOWLER'S ~15-20° + KNEES FLEXED (relaxes abdominal wall) — NOT high-Fowler's/upright/flat-with-legs-extended; (3) STERILE NORMAL-SALINE-MOISTENED gauze over viscera, KEEP MOIST; (4) DON'T push viscera back, NO dry gauze/cold/povidone-iodine/peroxide/tap water; (5) NPO; (6) SURGEON STAT + OR team; (7) 2 large-bore IVs + isotonic fluid + labs (CBC/BMP/coags/T&S) + broad-spectrum IV abx per order; (8) frequent vitals — watch shock/peritonitis; (9) prep OR (consent + anesthesia + transport); (10) document. Never delay the surgical call for charting/pain meds first. RN role: choreographed response.

labs.n: Definitive surgical management + post-op care + complication surveillance
labs.v: Definitive treatment is EMERGENT RETURN TO THE OR for repair: re-approximation of the fascia with retention or through-and-through sutures, peritoneal washout, hemostasis; mesh placement is considered for larger defects but often deferred in a clean-contaminated field. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics continue per surgeon. Post-operatively the patient typically returns with an NG tube for decompression (if ileus persists), an ABDOMINAL BINDER for support, and a SLOWED graded mobilization plan. Continue glycemic control (target the perioperative range per protocol), optimize NUTRITION (protein + vitamin C + zinc; dietitian consult), provide stool softeners to prevent straining, teach and have the patient PRACTICE SPLINTING the incision with a pillow during cough/sneeze/bowel movement, treat post-op pain effectively, and use incentive spirometry with gentle technique (deep breathing without bracing too tightly). COMPLICATION surveillance: re-dehiscence (especially if persistent cough, ileus, or infection is not addressed), surgical site infection (fever, increasing pain, erythema, purulent drainage), peritonitis (rigid abdomen, rebound, hypotension/tachycardia, lactate), ileus or bowel obstruction (links case-130), VTE (post-op high risk — prophylaxis per protocol), and pulmonary complications. Engage family and address anxiety; describe the next steps in plain language.
labs.r: Emergent OR = fascial re-approximation (retention/through-and-through sutures) + peritoneal washout + hemostasis ± mesh (often deferred in contaminated field). Continue broad-spectrum abx. Post-op: NG decompression if ileus, ABDOMINAL BINDER, GLYCEMIC control, NUTRITION (protein/vit C/zinc, dietitian), STOOL softeners, SPLINTING with cough/sneeze/BM, gentle IS, effective pain control. Surveillance: re-dehiscence, SSI, peritonitis, ileus/SBO ([[case-130]]), VTE, pulmonary. RN role: post-op care + complication surveillance.

labs.n: Prevention + discharge teaching + Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: PREVENTION: pre-operative optimization (nutrition, glycemic control, smoking cessation) when elective; ABDOMINAL BINDER for high-risk patients; SPLINTING the incision with a pillow during cough/sneeze/vomit/BM; treat ileus and post-op nausea (antiemetics); stool softeners to prevent straining; gentle incentive-spirometry technique; early but graded mobility; recognize and treat surgical site infection early. DISCHARGE TEACHING after repair: NO lifting greater than about 10 pounds for 6-8 weeks (or per surgeon); SPLINT the incision with cough/sneeze/bowel movements; wear the ABDOMINAL BINDER per surgeon orders for activity; take STOOL softeners and stay hydrated to avoid straining; recognize signs of RECURRENCE (popping/sudden drainage/wound separation) and INFECTION (fever, increasing pain, erythema, purulent drainage); maintain glycemic and nutritional plans; do NOT submerge the incision in baths or pools until cleared; report all warning signs to the provider; keep all follow-up appointments. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct emergency practice): the nurse did NOT push the viscera back into the abdomen; did NOT use a dry, cold, or chemical-soaked dressing (povidone-iodine/hydrogen peroxide/tap water); did NOT position the patient high-Fowler's or sitting upright or flat with legs extended; did NOT leave the patient alone to fetch supplies; did NOT give oral fluids or food; did NOT delay the surgical call to chart fully or medicate for pain first; and did NOT forget to document the precipitating cough and the timeline. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: instant recognition of the classic popping/sudden drainage cue; presence at the bedside; correct positioning (low-Fowler's with knees flexed); sterile saline-moistened gauze KEPT MOIST; NPO; STAT surgeon notification; large-bore IV access + isotonic resuscitation + labs/type and screen + broad-spectrum antibiotics; coordinated emergent OR; effective post-op care (binder, splinting, glycemia, nutrition, stool softeners); discharge teaching with teach-back; objective documentation. EXPECTED outcome (as here): the patient is stabilized, repaired safely, recovers without recurrence or major infection, and goes home with a robust prevention plan.
labs.r: PREVENTION: pre-op optimization + binder + SPLINTING with cough/sneeze/BM + treat ileus/nausea + stool softeners + gentle IS + early graded mobility + early SSI care. DISCHARGE: no lifting >10 lb x 6-8 wk, SPLINT, BINDER, stool softeners + hydration, recognize recurrence/SSI, glycemic + nutrition plans, no submerging incision, follow-up. Negatives confirm correct practice (didn't push viscera back, no dry/cold/chemical dressings, not high-Fowler's/sitting/flat-with-legs-extended, didn't leave patient, no oral intake, didn't delay surgeon call). Protective: recognition + bedside presence + correct positioning + sterile saline-moist gauze KEPT MOIST + NPO + surgeon STAT + IV/labs/abx + coordinated OR + post-op binder/splinting/glycemia/nutrition + teach-back + documentation.

labs.v: 2.8 g/dL (low — marker of perioperative undernutrition; contributes to poor wound healing)
12 mg/dL (low — better short-term nutrition marker; supports nutrition optimization plan)
12.4 x10^9/L (mildly elevated — possible contributing surgical site infection vs stress response)
188 mg/dL (perioperative hyperglycemia — impairs wound healing; tighten glycemic control)
11.0 g/dL (mildly low — baseline and trend after the event; type and screen in case transfusion needed)

nursesNote: Med-surg, 1015 — 68 y/o man POD 5 ex-lap + sigmoid colectomy for complicated diverticulitis (clean-contaminated). Risk profile: T2DM A1c 8.4, BMI 34, former 40-pk-yr smoker, borderline nutrition (albumin 2.8, prealbumin 12), post-op ileus + persistent cough. During forceful cough → sudden POPPING at midline incision + warm wet 'salmon-colored' drainage soaking dressing → on assessment incision SEPARATED with loops of BOWEL protruding through the fascial defect = WOUND DEHISCENCE WITH EVISCERATION (classic POD 5-12 presentation). RN choreographed response: (1) STAYED at bedside + called for help; (2) positioned LOW-FOWLER'S ~20° w/ KNEES FLEXED on pillow (relaxes abdominal wall — NOT high-Fowler's/sitting/flat-w/legs-extended); (3) covered exposed viscera with STERILE NORMAL-SALINE-MOISTENED gauze + KEPT MOIST (re-moistened w/ sterile saline as needed); (4) did NOT push viscera back, no dry/cold gauze, no povidone-iodine/peroxide/tap water; (5) NPO; (6) SURGEON paged STAT + OR/anesthesia/rapid-response activated; (7) 2 large-bore IVs + isotonic fluid + labs sent (CBC/BMP/coags/T&S — H/H 11.0, WBC 12.4, glucose 188) + broad-spectrum IV antibiotic per surgeon order (contamination risk); (8) frequent vitals (mild tachycardia → stabilizing); watch shock/peritonitis; (9) informed CONSENT for emergency repair signed, transport to OR arranged; (10) documented time + precipitating cough + wound findings + interventions + vitals + response. Pain mgmt per surgeon order (NOT delayed to medicate before calling). EMERGENT OR: fascial re-approximation w/ retention/through-and-through sutures + peritoneal washout + hemostasis; mesh deferred (contaminated field); NG decompression placed; abdominal BINDER applied; broad-spectrum IV abx continued. POST-OP: nutrition consult (protein + vit C + zinc), glycemic control tightened, SPLINTING with cough/sneeze/BM taught + practiced, abdominal binder for activity, stool softeners + hydration to avoid straining, graded mobilization, gentle IS, VTE prophylaxis, surveillance for re-dehiscence/SSI/peritonitis/ileus. DISCHARGE TEACHING (teach-back): no lifting >10 lb x 6-8 wk, SPLINT + BINDER + stool softeners + recognize warning signs of recurrence/SSI (popping/sudden drainage; fever/increasing pain/erythema/purulence) → call surgeon; glycemic + nutrition plans; no submerging incision in baths/pools until cleared; follow-up scheduled. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT push viscera back, did NOT use dry/cold/povidone/peroxide/tap-water dressings, did NOT position high-Fowler's/sitting/flat-with-legs-extended, did NOT leave the patient to get supplies, did NOT give PO intake, did NOT delay surgeon call for full charting or pain meds first. Family updated.
providerOrders: EMERGENCY: wound dehiscence with EVISCERATION on POD 5 after exploratory laparotomy with sigmoid colectomy — emergent return to OR for fascial repair

BEDSIDE NURSING ACTIONS (immediate): stay with the patient; position low-Fowler's (about 15-20 degrees) with knees flexed; cover exposed viscera with sterile gauze moistened with sterile normal saline and keep it moist (re-moisten as needed); do NOT push viscera back into the abdomen; do not use dry, cold, or antiseptic-soaked dressings (no povidone-iodine, hydrogen peroxide, or tap water on bowel); NPO immediately; obtain frequent vital signs; do not leave the bedside
NOTIFICATION: STAT page surgeon; activate rapid response/OR team per facility; alert anesthesia; notify family

ACCESS/FLUIDS/LABS: two large-bore peripheral IVs; isotonic crystalloid resuscitation per surgeon order; STAT CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, type and screen; lactate as indicated; ABG if shock features
ANTIBIOTICS: broad-spectrum IV antibiotic per surgeon order for the contamination/infection risk
PAIN: analgesia per surgeon order — do not delay the surgical call to medicate first; document
OR PREPARATION: confirm informed consent for emergency wound exploration and repair; verify NPO, baseline labs, and identifying details; arrange transport to OR

POSTOPERATIVE: NG decompression as ordered; abdominal binder; advance diet slowly per surgeon (clear liquids → as tolerated); continue IV antibiotics; glycemic control (insulin sliding scale + basal optimization for A1c 8.4); nutrition consult (protein, vitamin C, zinc); stool softeners; graded mobilization; gentle incentive spirometry technique with splinting; VTE prophylaxis per protocol; surveillance for re-dehiscence, SSI, peritonitis, ileus, and pulmonary complications

PREVENTION/EDUCATION (teach-back at discharge): no lifting greater than about 10 pounds for 6-8 weeks; SPLINT the incision with a pillow during cough/sneeze/bowel movement; wear the abdominal binder during activity per surgeon orders; take stool softeners and stay hydrated to avoid straining; recognize warning signs (popping/sudden drainage/wound separation, fever, increasing pain, erythema, purulent drainage) and contact the surgeon; do not submerge the incision in baths or pools until cleared; keep all follow-up appointments
DOCUMENTATION: time of event, precipitating event (cough/strain), wound findings, every intervention with time, vital signs and responses, notifications, OR transfer, and post-op care

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this wound emergency?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: A sudden 'POPPING' sensation during a forceful cough in a POD 5 abdominal-laparotomy patient with sudden warm wet 'salmon-colored' drainage is the CLASSIC presentation of wound dehiscence
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — classic story; recognize and act. RN role: instant recognition.

opts.text: Visible loops of BOWEL protruding through the fascial defect = EVISCERATION, a surgical emergency more urgent than dehiscence alone
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confirms the emergency and the OR pathway. RN role: visualize + act.

opts.text: Risk factors present: advanced age, T2DM with A1c 8.4, obesity (BMI 34), former heavy smoker, low albumin/prealbumin, ileus with persistent cough (increases intra-abdominal pressure)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — risk-factor cluster explains why this happened now. RN role: recognize the risk profile.

opts.text: Immediate nursing actions: STAY with the patient and call for help; LOW-FOWLER'S with KNEES FLEXED; cover viscera with STERILE NORMAL-SALINE-MOISTENED gauze and KEEP MOIST; NPO
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the choreographed bedside response. RN role: stay + position + sterile saline-moist gauze + NPO.

opts.text: Notify the SURGEON STAT; establish two large-bore IVs; send CBC/BMP/coags/type and screen; give broad-spectrum IV antibiotic per surgeon order; monitor vital signs for shock and peritonitis
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — escalation, resuscitation, and surveillance. RN role: notify + IV + labs + abx + monitor.

opts.text: Prepare for emergent OR — confirm informed consent, alert anesthesia, arrange transport; document time of event, precipitating cough, interventions, vitals, response
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — pathway to definitive repair + the record. RN role: OR prep + documentation.

opts.text: Push the bowel loops back into the abdomen and tape the wound closed before calling the surgeon
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Pushing viscera back contaminates and injures the bowel and delays surgical care.

opts.text: Place the patient flat with legs extended and apply a DRY sterile gauze to the bowel while you go find the supplies
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Flat-with-legs-extended raises intra-abdominal tension; dry gauze dries/necroses the bowel; never leave the patient. Low-Fowler's with knees flexed + sterile saline-moistened gauze + stay with the patient.

opts.text: Dehiscence = partial or complete separation of wound layers; evisceration = protrusion of viscera through the wound — evisceration is the more emergent of the two
opts.rat: CORRECT — the definitions. Foundation for triage and language.

opts.text: Common risk factors include advanced age, malnutrition, diabetes/poor glycemic control, obesity, smoking, immunosuppression, surgical site infection, and increased intra-abdominal pressure (cough/strain/ileus/ascites/distension)
opts.rat: CORRECT — risk-factor principle. Foundation for prevention.

opts.text: The classic timing is postoperative day 5-12; earlier suggests technical failure and later suggests infection or poor healing
opts.rat: CORRECT — timing principle. Foundation for clinical suspicion.

opts.text: The immediate bedside response is to stay with the patient, position low-Fowler's with knees flexed, cover viscera with sterile saline-moistened gauze kept moist, keep the patient NPO, and call the surgeon STAT
opts.rat: CORRECT — the response bundle. Foundation for safe action.

opts.text: Definitive treatment is emergent return to the OR for fascial repair with washout, hemostasis, and possible mesh (often deferred in contaminated fields); broad-spectrum IV antibiotics continue
opts.rat: CORRECT — surgical principle. Foundation for the pathway.

opts.text: Prevention focuses on glycemic and nutrition optimization, abdominal binders for high-risk patients, splinting with cough/sneeze/BM, stool softeners, treating ileus, gentle IS, and early but graded mobility
opts.rat: CORRECT — prevention principle. Foundation for recurrence-prevention teaching.

opts.text: Pushing the viscera back into the abdomen at the bedside is appropriate to prevent infection and avoid the OR
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Never push viscera back — causes contamination, mesenteric injury, peritonitis. The OR is required.

opts.text: The patient should be ambulated immediately at the bedside to 'shake the bowel back' and prevent further evisceration
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Ambulation increases intra-abdominal pressure and worsens evisceration. Low-Fowler's with knees flexed; NPO; OR.

opts.text: Stay with the patient; position LOW-FOWLER'S with KNEES FLEXED; cover exposed viscera with STERILE NORMAL-SALINE-MOISTENED gauze and KEEP MOIST; do NOT push viscera back
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The bedside response protects the viscera and the patient and is what only the nurse can do right now. RN role: stay + position + sterile saline-moist gauze.

opts.text: Call the SURGEON STAT and activate rapid response/OR team; keep the patient NPO; establish IV access and obtain vital signs
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Escalation and access are needed in parallel. RN role: notify + NPO + IV.

opts.text: Initiate isotonic IV fluid; send labs (CBC, BMP, coags, type and screen); give broad-spectrum IV antibiotic per surgeon order; monitor for shock and peritonitis
opts.rat: #3 — Stabilizes and prepares for the OR while watching for deterioration. RN role: resuscitation + labs + antibiotic + monitoring.

opts.text: Prepare for emergent OR — confirm informed consent, alert anesthesia, arrange transport
opts.rat: #4 — Moves the patient to definitive repair. RN role: OR prep.

opts.text: Document the event and coordinate post-op care + recurrence-prevention teaching (binder, splinting, glycemia, nutrition, stool softeners, signs of recurrence/SSI)
opts.rat: #5 — Closes the loop and prevents recurrence. RN role: documentation + post-op + prevention.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this evisceration.

opts.text: Stay with the patient and call for help; position low-Fowler's (about 15-20 degrees) with knees flexed on a pillow
opts.rat: Reduces abdominal-wall tension and protects the patient. RN role: stay + position.

opts.text: Cover the exposed viscera with sterile gauze moistened with sterile normal saline and keep it moist (re-moisten with sterile saline as needed)
opts.rat: Protects the viscera from drying/necrosis and contamination. RN role: sterile saline-moistened gauze, kept moist.

opts.text: NPO immediately; call the surgeon STAT and activate rapid response/OR; alert anesthesia
opts.rat: Anticipates OR and mobilizes the team. RN role: NPO + notification + activation.

opts.text: Establish two large-bore IVs; run isotonic crystalloid per orders; send CBC/BMP/coags/type and screen; give broad-spectrum IV antibiotic per surgeon order
opts.rat: Supports resuscitation, OR readiness, and infection prevention. RN role: IV + labs + abx.

opts.text: Confirm informed consent for emergency repair; obtain frequent vital signs; arrange OR transport; document the event and timeline
opts.rat: Completes the pathway to definitive treatment with the record. RN role: OR prep + monitoring + documentation.

opts.text: Plan post-op care (NG decompression if ileus, abdominal binder, glycemic and nutritional optimization, stool softeners, splinting, gentle IS, VTE prophylaxis, complication surveillance) and recurrence-prevention discharge teaching
opts.rat: Sustains recovery and prevents recurrence. RN role: post-op + prevention + teaching.

opts.text: Push the bowel back into the abdomen and tape the wound closed before calling the surgeon
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — contaminates and injures the bowel and delays surgical care.

opts.text: Apply DRY gauze to the bowel and leave the bedside to find supplies
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — dry gauze dries/necroses the bowel and the patient should not be left alone.

opts.text: Place the patient in HIGH-Fowler's or upright sitting to make the wound easier to dress
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — increases intra-abdominal tension and worsens evisceration; use low-Fowler's with knees flexed.

opts.text: Give the patient oral fluids 'because he is thirsty' and pour povidone-iodine or hydrogen peroxide over the bowel to 'sterilize' it
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — NPO for the OR; antiseptics on bowel cause chemical injury. Use sterile normal saline only.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stay with the patient and call for help; position low-Fowler's with knees flexed; cover viscera with sterile saline-moistened gauze and keep it moist; do NOT push viscera back
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. The bedside response that only the nurse can do right now. RN role: stay + position + sterile saline-moist gauze.

opts.text: Call the surgeon STAT and activate rapid response/OR; keep the patient NPO; establish IV access; obtain vital signs
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Escalation, NPO, and access in parallel. RN role: notify + NPO + IV.

opts.text: Initiate isotonic IV fluid; send labs (CBC, BMP, coags, type and screen); give broad-spectrum IV antibiotic per surgeon order; monitor for shock and peritonitis
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Resuscitation, OR readiness, and surveillance. RN role: fluids + labs + abx + monitor.

opts.text: Prepare for emergent OR — confirm informed consent, alert anesthesia, arrange transport
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Pathway to definitive repair. RN role: OR prep.

opts.text: Document the event objectively; coordinate post-op care (NG decompression, abdominal binder, glycemic and nutritional optimization, splinting, stool softeners) and discharge teaching
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Documentation + post-op + prevention complete the response. RN role: document + post-op + teaching.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: The nurse stayed with the patient, positioned low-Fowler's with knees flexed, covered viscera with sterile saline-moistened gauze kept moist, called the surgeon STAT, established IV access, and kept NPO — vitals stabilize while awaiting OR transport
opts.rat: Correct choreographed response that protects viscera and patient. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Emergent OR completed fascial re-approximation with peritoneal washout; IV antibiotics continued; the patient returned with an NG tube and abdominal binder, stable
opts.rat: Definitive repair with appropriate post-op support. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Post-op care included glycemic and nutritional optimization, stool softeners, splinting practice with cough/sneeze/BM, abdominal binder for activity, and graded mobilization without recurrence
opts.rat: Sustained recovery with recurrence prevention. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Discharge teaching was teach-back verified — no lifting greater than 10 pounds for 6-8 weeks, splinting, binder use, stool softeners, and warning signs of recurrence/SSI
opts.rat: Family + patient prepared for safe home recovery. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the nurse pushed the bowel back into the abdomen and taped the wound closed before calling; the patient developed peritonitis and sepsis
opts.rat: ⚠️ Pushing viscera back contaminates and injures the bowel. Demonstrates the absolute rule against this.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — a DRY gauze was placed over the bowel and not moistened; bowel sections dried and required resection in the OR
opts.rat: ⚠️ Dry gauze caused necrosis. Demonstrates the sterile-saline-moist-gauze-kept-moist rule.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the patient was placed flat with legs extended and then briefly left alone to fetch supplies; bowel further protruded and he had a vasovagal collapse
opts.rat: ⚠️ Flat-with-legs-extended raised tension; leaving the patient violated the bedside-presence rule. Demonstrates low-Fowler's with knees flexed and staying with the patient.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the nurse delayed the surgeon call to medicate for pain first and to complete a long charting note; OR transfer was significantly delayed
opts.rat: ⚠️ The surgical call comes first. Demonstrates the priority of escalation; pain medication and charting come after the call.

opts.text: In the minutes after the bedside response, with sterile moist gauze in place, low-Fowler's with knees flexed, NPO, IV in, and the OR being mobilized — vital signs and visceral position are stable while awaiting transport
opts.rat: An expected stable trajectory while waiting for the OR; continue current management and reassess frequently. Unchanged is appropriate here.

opts.text: A year later, with splinting, binder use during heavy activity, ongoing glycemic and nutrition plans, smoking remaining quit, and follow-up complete — the patient has not had a recurrence
opts.rat: Sustained prevention worked. Ideal longer-term outcome.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Basic Care and Comfort, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
